Feedback.

think there was some like the use of MDashes, the use of however, that I think actually conflict.

you.

stuff like that, I feel like it would be caught by a human reviewer as the last step.

that.

So the way the articles are just generated is, well.
Sort of, you add a new topic, and we'll sort of give high-level guidelines in terms of the persona, the length, keyword, any, like, specific content requirements around positioning.
And so this is everything that goes into the assignment generation.
And from there, it goes through a multi-stage process, and these kind of pull from different models.
Like, think, depending on what pipeline we have, like, sometimes it pulls from perplexity, sometimes exit using different models for research.
And then it will draft the article based on kind of those parameters we gave it and its research on the topic.
And then it will add kind of internal links based on your blog.
And then we have a number of steps that are sort of checking.
So, validating your writing guidelines, again, you know, this is where we had talked about adding another...
Step to fact check it based on u.com kind of product capabilities.

like adding specific product checking stages where anytime it makes a claim about the product, it will kind of isolate that section and then go back and check it against a product capabilities document.
So, but at the very end, you know, we have a human editor who's running this state, this whole process.
And at the end, she does review all of the content and check it.
So it always, always have a human reviewer at the end.
I think some of it depends on, you know, the client's needs versus, and some of it's just getting familiar with the product, right?
Like catching things like mistakes in the product.
And some of it, too, is like understanding each client's writing, writing style.
So understanding which things to check.
Because I think things like repetition and the TLDR and the intro.
Some of the kind of style right now is because we found the TLDR is useful for kind of capturing LLM traffic.
There is just a certain amount of repetition that goes into that because the, you know, repeating the info in the intro is kind of good for SEO.
And then that TLDR is kind of useful for that chunk is useful for AI citations and LLM traffic.
So some of that is just not catching that because that repetition is just like a little bit the way things are right now.
It's like not ideal from a human, I think.
I think we've talked about it a little bit.
It's not ideal from kind of a human experience from reading, but it has been shown to be helpful in terms of LLM traffic.
So some of it's just kind of what's the right fit for your brand there.
But there is a human who is reviewing all of this and kind of making these.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Updates and improvements.
So just to kind of set you at ease, it is being reviewed.

And so I think we are, our goal is to get to the point where you're not, like, I hesitate to say, like, you should just go in and make the changes, because I think the goal is for you not to have to do that.
But I do think for also the goal is to kind of get content published, know, reach a reach a bar of quality and get it published so that you get to start seeing results.

So I think some of the question, too, is, you know, where is that, where is crossing that bar of quality, where it's kind of good enough versus changes that I think are not, that are kind of stylistically preferential, but might be really hard to create rules and writing guidelines based on, and whether or not those updates, I think, kind of improve the experience for either human readers or for kind of LLM.
It's a little bit of question, too.
Yeah.
I could also, you know, take that into account and say, like, my edits will be strictly for content.
And then once, you know, it's through the whole pipe, whatever I edit after before publishing can be stylistic so that it helps kind of flesh out the process before it gets to me.
That maybe is the best way to do it.

But I'm also curious how other clients that are, like, B2B tech-focused have dealt with, like, an SME step or, like, how that comes into play more just because I obviously don't have all the technical expertise of what you.com does.
It's, you know, I'm not going to expect your team to know that as well, but I rely on the internal experts here.
So, if that needs to be a step that happens on our side after the fact and doesn't get brought back in, like, where that falls and what you've seen work for other clients.
Yeah.
I think it depends a little bit on what they're reviewing for and what stage.
Like, we definitely have a lot of clients where SME review is part
The process.
And I think it often will go two different ways.
I think often the most successful ones are when, like, the subject matter experts are given, like, more, I think, specific guidelines to really review for kind of accuracy.

And because I have found that sometimes people get derailed by, like, style or, like, not totally understanding the purpose of SEO content, where it really has to match the search intent, where it can be kind of very templated in terms of what sections you should be including.
So I think sometimes that can delay the process.
And when people sort of aren't given, like, specific guidelines on what to be reviewing.
But I think then we can do it in one of two ways, where either you send it back to us to kind of make those updates, or depending on, like, the level of the updates, where you update.
Update and then kind of publish yourselves.
We've done it both ways with clients.

I do think that it can be useful to send it back to us, even if it creates a little bit more of a delay, because it sort of means if there's inaccuracies in terms of how we're positioning the products and more subtle things, it allows us to kind of catch those and build them back into those guidelines and those products.
Project Positioning Artifacts, so we're hopefully not making, you know, getting the same feedback twice.
So I would say that it just depends, like, a little bit, I think, on how deep the reviews are and what kind of issues they're catching.
But at least at the beginning, I think it could be useful to send it back to us to make sure we're capturing any needed updates there.
Okay.

will like...

I mean, I think the goal would be to, in an ideal state, we're sort of taking any insights and documentation and like expertise you have.

The more we can find ways to kind of like take that out of the brains of your subject matter experts and put it into documentation so that we are running checks kind of against that, which is why I'm always like asking for existing documentation, I think the more we can.
Do that.

That's kind of the ideal state where the content becomes good enough that like you, A, you feel confident in publishing it and B, like a subject matter check becomes much more of a, just like a quick gut check and not like an in-depth review process.
I think sometimes for highly technical content, we may kind of change the actual pipeline we use.
